verses.text: When Samuel was old, that he made his sons judges over Israel. Now the name of his firstborn was Joel; and the name of his second, Abiah: they were judges in Beersheba. His sons walked not in his ways, but turned aside after lucre, and took bribes, and perverted judgment. Guidance: The chapter opens with a quiet tragedy: Samuel's sons fail. Joel ('the LORD is God') and Abiah ('the LORD is my Father') wear theological names but live mercenary lives. They take bribes; they bend the law. The narrator is honest about the most influential prophet's most painful chapter: his own household. Samuel had been an answer to Hannah's prayer; his sons are not an answer to his. The pattern Eli once embodied is repeating itself in the man who replaced Eli. Even faithful fathers do not automatically produce faithful sons. The era of judges has now collapsed because its last judge cannot pass it on.

Then all the elders of Israel gathered themselves together, and came to Samuel unto Ramah, and said unto him, Behold, thou art old, and thy sons walk not in thy ways: now make us a king to judge us like all the nations. Guidance: The elders' request has real legitimacy and real rot. Legitimately: Samuel is old, his sons are crooked, succession is in crisis. Rotten: 'like all the nations.' That phrase was already on God's lips in Deut 17:14 as the trigger condition for legitimate kingship; the request itself is not forbidden. But the heart of the request is wrong. They want a king not to lead them in covenant but to make them look like everyone else. The desire for normalcy in the eyes of the neighbors is the chapter's deepest sin. Israel's distinctness was its glory; they ask to trade it for a crown.

Then all the elders of Israel gathered themselves together, and came to Samuel unto Ramah, and said unto him, Behold, thou art old, and thy sons walk not in thy ways: now make us a king to judge us like all the nations. Guidance: The elders' request has real legitimacy and real rot. Legitimately: Samuel is old, his sons are crooked, succession is in crisis. Rotten: 'like all the nations.' That phrase was already on God's lips in Deut 17:14 as the trigger condition for legitimate kingship; the request itself is not forbidden. But the heart of the request is wrong. They want a king not to lead them in covenant but to make them look like everyone else. The desire for normalcy in the eyes of the neighbors is the chapter's deepest sin. Israel's distinctness was its glory; they ask to trade it for a crown.

But the thing displeased Samuel, when they said, Give us a king to judge us. And Samuel prayed unto the LORD. And the LORD said unto Samuel, Hearken unto the voice of the people in all that they say unto thee: for they have not rejected thee, but they have rejected me, that I should not reign over them. According to all the works which they have done since the day that I brought them up out of Egypt even unto this day, wherewith they have forsaken me, and served other gods, so do they also unto thee. Now therefore hearken unto their voice: howbeit yet protest solemnly unto them, and shew them the manner of the king that shall reign over them. Guidance: Samuel takes the request personally — and his hurt is real. The LORD gently reframes it: 'they have not rejected thee, but they have rejected me.' Sometimes the bruises a leader feels are actually for God, not for the leader; pastoring well includes recognizing whose throne is actually being refused. The LORD makes a stunning concession: 'Hearken unto their voice.' He will give them what they ask for, but with full disclosure. Israel will have its king; they will also have a transparent warning about what kings cost. This is permission, not endorsement — the LORD's permissive will accommodating what His perfect will did not require.

Samuel told all the words of the LORD unto the people that asked of him a king. And he said, This will be the manner of the king that shall reign over you: He will take your sons, and appoint them for himself, for his chariots, and to be his horsemen; and some shall run before his chariots. And he will appoint him captains over thousands, and captains over fifties; and will set them to ear his ground, and to reap his harvest, and to make his instruments of war, and instruments of his chariots. And he will take your daughters to be confectionaries, and to be cooks, and to be bakers. And he will take your fields, and your vineyards, and your oliveyards, even the best of them, and give them to his servants. And he will take the tenth of your seed, and of your vineyards, and give to his officers, and to his servants. And he will take your menservants, and your maidservants, and your goodliest young men, and your asses, and put them to his work. He will take the tenth of your sheep: and ye shall be his servants. And ye shall cry out in that day because of your king which ye shall have chosen you; and the LORD will not hear you in that day. Guidance: Samuel's manner-of-the-king speech is a king-cost forecast in plain language. The verb 'will take' repeats eight times. Take sons for chariots. Take daughters for kitchens. Take fields, vineyards, olive groves — the best. Take a tenth of grain and flocks. Take servants and animals. The verb is brutal: nothing offered, everything seized. Solomon's later reign will read like a literal fulfillment. The kicker is the last line: 'ye shall cry out… and the LORD will not hear you.' Israel had been delivered every time it cried in Judges; it will not be delivered from the consequences of this choice. Some prayers God refuses to answer because the answer would interrupt what we asked Him for. Choose your kings carefully; you may get them.

Nevertheless the people refused to obey the voice of Samuel; and they said, Nay; but we will have a king over us; that we also may be like all the nations; and that our king may judge us, and go out before us, and fight our battles. And Samuel heard all the words of the people, and he rehearsed them in the ears of the LORD. And the LORD said to Samuel, Hearken unto their voice, and make them a king. And Samuel said unto the men of Israel, Go ye every man unto his city. Guidance: 'Nay; but we will have a king.' Three things they want: be like the nations, have a judge, have a war-leader. The first is the deepest wound — they have stopped wanting to be a holy nation. The chapter's quiet sadness is in Samuel's verb: he 'rehearsed' (literally 'spoke into the ears of') what the people said to the LORD. The prophet does not editorialize; he reports. And the LORD's reply is the same word He gave at v7 and v9: 'Hearken; make them a king.' He sends them home for now. Saul is coming. The choice has been made. The chapter ends in dispersal — 'go ye every man unto his city' — the same words Joshua used at the end of his covenant ceremony (Josh 24:28). The difference is that one ceremony enthroned the LORD and the other has just dethroned Him. Israel goes home, in both cases, to wait for what they have asked for.
